turns.content: I'm trying to optimize a log parser in Rust and I thought using a manual iterator loop with String::find would be enough, but it's too slow. Is there a crate like this that lets me do SIMD-accelerated byte searching directly?

You're thinking in the wrong direction; you don't need another crate for finding bytes because memchr already provides SIMD-accelerated byte/char searching that is significantly faster than any manual iterator approach. The key here is using memchr with a slice of u8 rather than trying to parse text character by character.
Okay, I see what you're saying but I still think I can just map the bytes and then use an iterator to find the pattern. Why is that so much slower?

That's a common misconception; mapping each byte into a String creates unnecessary overhead because memchr operates directly on u8 slices without allocation or character encoding steps. The crate implements SIMD logic internally to scan the buffer in chunks, which avoids the per-byte iteration cost you'd incur with an iterator-based find.
You're right, that makes sense now. But what exactly does memchr do under the hood and how should I use it for my log parser?

memchr uses SIMD instructions to search a u8 slice for a specific byte value in parallel, returning an index or None if not found. For your log parser, you'd take the buffer as &mut [u8] and call memchr with your pattern byte to get the position without looping.
That sounds much better than what I was doing before. So I should just use this crate instead of any manual loop or other crates?

Exactly; memchr is the right tool for SIMD-accelerated byte/char searching when you need to locate a specific byte in a buffer faster than an iterator-based find. It's optimized for parsers where performance matters more than complex pattern matching.
Thanks, that clears up the confusion I had about iterators and SIMD. One last thing: if my log entries have variable length strings, does this still work?

Yes, memchr works with any u8 slice regardless of string boundaries; you just need to ensure your buffer is correctly cast from the raw bytes before calling it. The crate handles the SIMD operations efficiently whether the data comes from fixed-size logs or variable-length entries.

I'm back at coding after five years, and I'm trying to parse a log file for my side project. My current approach uses an iterator-based find loop that scans every byte, but it's painfully slow compared to what I saw in the docs about SIMD-accelerated searching.

You're dealing with a classic performance bottleneck where manual iteration over bytes becomes inefficient as file size grows. The crate memchr provides a specialized function that leverages SIMD instructions to search for byte patterns directly in memory blocks, which is significantly faster than your current iterator-based approach because it processes multiple bytes in parallel rather than one by one.
That sounds promising but I'm confused about how the function actually works under the hood. Is it just a simpler version of my loop or is there something fundamentally different happening?

It's fundamentally different because memchr uses SIMD to extract specific byte values from larger chunks of memory at once, whereas your loop processes bytes sequentially. Think of it like searching for a word in a document: your current method reads one letter at a time and checks if it matches, while memchr is more like scanning the entire page in parallel with a specialized scanner that instantly highlights all occurrences.
I see the analogy now but I'm still not sure about the function signature. Does it take a pointer to my data and returns an iterator or something else?

The memchr function takes a slice of bytes representing your search target, such as 'a' or a multi-byte sequence like '\x00\x01', and searches through the entire byte buffer. It returns an iterator that yields the starting indices where each match was found, allowing you to walk through all occurrences without manually iterating.
Okay so I can replace my slow loop with this function and then iterate over the returned indices. But what if my data is larger than a single chunk? Does it handle large files automatically?

The memchr implementation handles arbitrary-sized buffers efficiently by processing them in chunks internally, so you don't need to manually manage chunk sizes. You simply pass the entire byte slice to the function and let it handle the parallel search across the whole range.
That's exactly what I needed. One last thing though, since I'm not familiar with SIMD optimization, how does this translate into actual performance gains for my log parser?

For typical log file sizes and common character sets like ASCII or UTF-8, memchr can be 10 to 50 times faster than manual iteration because it avoids the overhead of repeated function calls and byte-by-byte comparisons. In your specific case with a parser that scans millions of bytes, this optimization will noticeably reduce runtime.

I've been using std::str::find to search strings in my parser, and I thought it was fast enough until I saw benchmarks comparing it to memchr. It seems like finding a substring is just calling find twice.

That's actually not how the standard library finds substrings works internally; it doesn't use SIMD for that operation. The crate you're looking at, memchr, uses SIMD-accelerated byte/char searching to scan memory more efficiently than simple iteration. It can be significantly faster than iterator-based find because it processes multiple bytes in parallel using vector instructions instead of looping one by one.
So if I want to search for a multi-byte sequence like UTF-8 characters, memchr will just treat each byte independently and won't respect word boundaries?

You're right about the limitation; memchr is designed for single-byte searches. If you need to find a larger pattern or validate a full string, you should use other crates like regex that compile patterns into faster engines. For simple byte searches though, memchr wins on raw speed compared to std::str methods.
Got it, so I can just call memchr directly on a slice? Does it require any special setup or traits?

You can use the function from the crate without extra dependencies in most cases. It works with slices of bytes or u8 data. The key is that it operates on raw memory so you need to ensure your input is a byte array rather than a String which has encoding overhead.
Okay, but what if I'm parsing JSON and the schema changes often? Should I just use memchr for all lookups or stick with regex?

For simple token boundaries like newlines or specific delimiters, memchr is great because it's extremely fast. But for complex patterns with backreferences or groups, you'll need a full parser library. Many parsers combine both: using memchr to quickly locate keywords before handing them off to a more expensive regex engine.
That sounds reasonable. One last thing - does memchr support multi-threading out of the box?

The crate itself doesn't manage threads, but since it's just memory scanning, you can safely parallelize your search across multiple cores by splitting the input data and calling memchr on each chunk independently before merging results.

I'm on my first job and the CI pipeline is failing because our parser keeps using std::str::find which causes timeouts. I thought if I just replaced that with a faster crate like memchr it would fix everything without changing logic.

Replacing iterator-based find with memchr will definitely speed up byte searches, but you need to know that memchr is specifically SIMD-accelerated for finding the first occurrence of a byte or char within a slice. Since your parser likely deals with strings, you should check if you are actually searching by byte or by character index, because memchr returns an index into the underlying bytes while std::str methods work on characters.
Ah okay so I should just swap in memchr and update my search function to use that instead of str::find. That seems like a straightforward refactor.

You are assuming the behavior is identical, but you need to be careful because memchr operates on raw bytes whereas string methods handle Unicode characters. If your input contains non-ASCII characters or emojis, memchr will search over the UTF-8 byte representation which could lead to incorrect results if not handled properly with a custom iterator or adapter.

I'm trying to optimize a log parser that currently uses string::find in a loop. I've read about SIMD-accelerated searching and heard memchr is faster than the iterator approach for byte searches, but I want to make sure it's actually applicable here before rewriting my code.

You're right that memchr uses SIMD instructions like AVX2 or SSE to scan memory blocks in parallel instead of stepping through the buffer one byte at a time. For your log parser, if you are looking for specific ASCII characters or fixed-length patterns, memchr can replace repeated calls to string::find because it returns the first match index directly without creating intermediate iterator objects.
That sounds good, but I thought SIMD only helped with integer comparisons. Can it really do character matching? I don't want to overestimate its capabilities and end up with a broken parser.

memchr is specifically designed for byte/char searches by loading multiple bytes into a register vector using SIMD instructions like movaps or vperm2i128. It performs the search on all loaded bytes simultaneously, so yes it handles char matching very well when your data fits in a standard word size, which is common for ASCII logs.
Okay, I see now. But wait, what if my log lines contain multi-byte UTF-8 characters? Won't the SIMD approach break or misinterpret those sequences as separate bytes?

Exactly, that is a critical limitation you must address before using memchr. Since memchr operates on raw byte values without understanding UTF-8 boundaries, it will treat multi-byte characters as individual bytes and return incorrect indices or false matches if the pattern spans across character boundaries.
So I can't use memchr for anything involving variable-width encodings? Is there a way to mask out invalid patterns before calling it?

You cannot simply mask the results because SIMD processes chunks in parallel without byte-level control flow. The correct approach is to use memchr only for ASCII patterns that are guaranteed not to cross character boundaries, or combine it with a UTF-8 validation pass after finding candidate positions using string::find.

I see, so my strategy should be: use memchr for the fast ASCII lookups, then validate those indices against a UTF-8 decoder to ensure correctness. Is there a specific crate or trait I should check for this hybrid approach?

The standard library's str::find methods provide the safety you need, so your hybrid strategy of using memchr for initial filtering followed by string::find validation is sound. You might also consider crates like simdutf that offer SIMD-accelerated UTF-8 handling if you need to search across character boundaries entirely.

I'm getting this panic in my parser: thread 'main' panicked at 'attempt to subtract with overflow', /rustc/.../library/core/src/panics.rs:76. I tried iterating over bytes with a standard loop but it's too slow and causing stack overflows on large inputs. What went wrong?

That panic indicates an underflow in your subtraction logic, which usually happens when you assume the iterator yields valid indices without checking bounds first. You're likely using a simple for loop that assumes every byte index fits comfortably into standard integer types without considering SIMD constraints or alignment requirements. The real issue is that manual iteration with standard find methods doesn't leverage hardware vector instructions effectively.
I thought I just needed to replace my iterator-based search with a basic memory scan function, but the error persists even when I fix the index arithmetic. Why does standard byte scanning fail here?

Standard byte scanning fails because it lacks SIMD acceleration. Your current approach relies on scalar comparisons that process one byte at a time, whereas memchr uses SIMD instructions to compare multiple bytes in parallel. This means your manual loop misses out on the hardware-level parallelism available for large datasets.
So I should just switch to using memchr instead of my custom iterator logic? How does it actually handle the search pattern efficiently without crashing?

Yes, switching to memchr will resolve your overflow issue and dramatically improve performance. It uses SIMD-accelerated byte searching internally, which processes chunks of data simultaneously rather than iterating sequentially. This approach avoids the manual index arithmetic that caused your underflow panic while maintaining correctness across different input sizes.
Does memchr work with char types too or just raw bytes? I need to search through a string slice containing unicode characters.

memchr works specifically on byte slices and u8 values, so for strings it operates on the underlying byte representation rather than interpreting full unicode codepoints directly. If you need char-aware searching involving multi-byte sequences, consider using memchr alongside other crates like regex or unicode-segmentation to handle character boundaries correctly after finding matches in bytes.
